Index › Return Attribution and Benchmarks › Module 1: Return Attribution

1.11 | Return Attribution Analysis at Multiple Levels

Module 1: Return Attribution1.11 | Return Attribution Analysis at Multiple Levels

 Learning Outcomes

The candidate should be able to:

  • distinguish between the effects of sponsors’ and managers’ investment decisions
  • calculate and interpret attribution analysis at different levels: plan sponsor, portfolio manager, country, industrial sector, and individual security

The example used to demonstrate return attribution so far is a single country model in which the investment process allocates weight to industry sectors and then selects securities within each of those industry sectors. The same return attribution formulas for allocation and selection can be used at multiple levels in the entire decision process to evaluate different decisions. Consider an example in which the top level is the fund sponsor (e.g., a university endowment or a defined-benefit pension plan sponsor). At the fund sponsor level, the first decision might be to allocate a certain weight to asset classes—the strategic asset allocation. If the fund sponsor does not manage funds internally, they would delegate a second decision in the investment process to the investment managers who could decide on any tactical deviations from the strategic asset allocation. And the sponsor might choose a number of portfolio managers to manage against specific mandates within a given asset class. (Attribution at the sponsor level is sometimes called macro attribution; attribution at the portfolio manager level is sometimes called micro attribution.)

Using the investment decision process just described, the allocation formula can be used to measure the tactical asset allocation decision of the sponsor against its own strategic benchmark, which could be a weighted average of appropriate asset class indexes using the strategic asset allocation weights or a representation of the sponsor’s liabilities, as in the case of many pension funds. The selection formula can be used to measure the added value of the performance of each of the portfolio managers relative to their assigned benchmarks. To the fund sponsor, the selection of portfolio managers is in effect a selection decision in the return attribution analysis performed at the highest level.

When we calculate return attribution, we have the opportunity to choose which level(s) to analyze. Consider the example of a fund sponsor who hires two investment managers for the equity portion of the fund. The overall fund has an equity allocation benchmark of:

  • 50% Large-Cap Value Equities

  • 25% Small-Cap Value Equities

  • 25% Large-Cap Growth Equities

For this overall allocation, the fund sponsor hires Value Portfolio Manager to manage the large-cap and small-cap value allocations and Growth Portfolio Manager to manage the growth equity allocation. For a specific time period, this arrangement results in the performance shown in Exhibit 20.

 Exhibit 20Performance of Value and Growth Equity Managers
Exhibit 20: Performance of Value and Growth Equity Managers
Fund Weight Fund Return ベンチマークウェイト(Benchmark Weight) ベンチマークリターン(Benchmark Return)
Total 100% 0.95 100% −0.03
バリューポートフォリオマネージャー(Value Portfolio Manager) 78% 0.99 75% 0.32
小型株バリュー株(Small-cap value equities) 20% 2.39 25% 1.52
大型株バリュー株(Large-cap value equities) 58% 0.51 50% −0.28
グロースポートフォリオマネージャー(Growth Portfolio Manager) 22% 0.82 25% −1.08
大型株グロース株(Large-cap growth equities) 22% 0.82 25% -1.08

You will note that this fund outperformed its total benchmark by 98 bps (95 bps compared with –3 bps). You will also note that the fund sponsor has allocated 78% of the total fund to the Value Portfolio Manager, exceeding the benchmark weight of 75%, with the remaining 22% allocated to the Growth Portfolio Manager, below the benchmark 25%. This overall weighting decision is that of the fund sponsor.

Within each of these total allocations, the portfolio manager has discretion about how to invest the assets. The managers have full discretion over the securities purchased, within the particular market segment benchmark. For this example, we assume that the Value Manager has full discretion to allocate between market cap segments—that is, how to divide the total 78% allocation to value equities between small cap and large cap.

Before we calculate our return attribution effects, we need to decide whom we want to evaluate. Do we want to evaluate the decisions of the portfolio manager, or those of the fund sponsor? In this first set of return attribution results, we will evaluate the portfolio manager’s decisions. To do this, we will calculate the return attribution effects (using the BF approach) at the segment level (e.g., small-cap value, large-cap value, and large-cap growth). Using the earlier formulas, where:

Allocation = (wi – Wi)(Bi – B)
Selection + Interaction = Wi(Ri – Bi) + (wi – Wi)(Ri – Bi)

We can calculate the attribution effects for the small-cap value equities:

Allocation = (20% – 25%)[1.52 – (–0.03)] = –0.08
Selection + Interaction = [(25%)(2.39 – 1.52)] + [(20% – 25%)(2.39 – 1.52)] = 0.17

Using the same approach for large-cap value equities and large-cap growth equities yields the results shown in Exhibit 21. (Note that numbers are shown to two decimal places and may not sum because of rounding.)

 Exhibit 21Segment Level Return Attribution
Exhibit 21: Segment Level Return Attribution
Return attribution
(segment level)		 Fund Weight 選択+相互作用(Selection + Interaction) 配分(Allocation) Total
Total 100% 1.05 −0.07 0.98
Value Portfolio Manager 78% 0.63 −0.10 0.53
Small-cap value equities 20% 0.17 −0.08 0.10
Large-cap value equities 58% 0.46 −0.02 0.44
Growth Portfolio Manager 22% 0.42 0.03 0.45
Large-cap growth equities 22% 0.42 0.03 0.45

In Exhibit 21, the attribution results in bold are calculated at the segment level. The attribution results at the next level above, the Value Portfolio Manager and Growth Portfolio Manager, are sums of the segment level results. For example, the allocation effect for the Value Portfolio Manager is equal to −0.08 + −0.02 = −0.10.

Because we are calculating the effects at the segment level and rolling up (summing) to a total for each manager, we are able to evaluate the specific decisions of the portfolio managers and their impact on the total fund. The total 98 bps of outperformance at the overall fund level is almost entirely the result of positive security selection decisions (105 bps in total). The decision of the Value Manager to underweight small cap in favor of large cap was a poor decision because the small-cap value benchmark outperformed the total benchmark (1.52% versus –0.03%) and the large-cap value benchmark underperformed the total benchmark (−0.28% versus –0.03%). Therefore, we calculate a negative allocation effect of –7 bps.

別途、ファンドスポンサー(fund sponsor)の判断(decisions)も評価できる。このために、展示図20(Exhibit 20)の同じウェイト(weights)とリターンデータ(returns data)を用いて2回目のリターンアトリビューション(return attribution)分析を行うが、今回はセグメントレベル(segment level)ではなく、ポートフォリオマネージャー(portfolio manager)レベルで計算する。例えば、バリューポートフォリオマネージャー(Value Portfolio Manager)の場合、影響(effects)は次のように計算される。

Allocation = (78% – 75%)[0.32 – (–0.03)] = 0.01
Selection + Interaction = [(75%)(0.99 – 0.32)] + [(78% – 75%)(0.99 – 0.32)] = 0.52

同じ方法でグロースポートフォリオマネージャー(Growth Portfolio Manager)についても計算し、展示図22(Exhibit 22)に示される結果を得る。

 Exhibit 22Return Attribution at the Portfolio Manager Level
展示図22: ポートフォリオマネージャー(portfolio manager)レベルでのリターンアトリビューション(return attribution)
リターンアトリビューション(ポートフォリオマネージャー(portfolio manager)レベル) Selection + Interaction 配分(Allocation) Total
Total 0.94 0.04 0.98
Value Portfolio Manager 0.52 0.01 0.53
Small-cap value equities – – –
Large-cap value equities – – –
Growth Portfolio Manager 0.42 0.03 0.45
Large-cap growth equities – – –

マネージャーレベル(manager level)でのみ結果を計算したため、セグメントレベル(segment level)でのリターンアトリビューション効果は存在しないことに注意。測定する判断(decisions)が異なるため、アトリビューション結果(attribution results)も異なる。依然として総合的な正の証券選択効果(positive security selection effect)は存在し(94bps)、予想通りの結果となる。しかし、総配分効果(total allocation effect)は負ではなく正である。これは異なる配分判断(allocation decision)を評価しているためである。この場合、価値株(value equities)を重視(overweight)し、グロース株(growth equities)を軽視(underweight)するというファンドスポンサー(fund sponsor)の判断の影響を計算している。配分効果(allocation)は4bpsの正となり、価値株(value equities)のベンチマーク(benchmark)が総ベンチマーク(total benchmark)をアウトパフォーム(outperform)し、グロース株(growth equities)のベンチマークが総ベンチマーク(total benchmark)をアンダーパフォーム(underperform)したことを反映している。

リターンアトリビューション(return attribution)分析は、最も一般的にはポートフォリオ(portfolio)の合意されたベンチマーク(agreed benchmark)に基づいて計算される。しかし、同じまたは類似の投資戦略(investment strategy)を用いて、あるポートフォリオ(portfolio)を別のポートフォリオに対してアトリビュート(attribution)することも十分に可能である。このような分析の目的は、同じポートフォリオマネージャー(portfolio manager)が同じ投資判断プロセス(investment decision process)を用いて運用する2つのポートフォリオ(portfolios)間で予期せぬリターンの差を説明することかもしれない。

次のレベルに掘り下げると、ポートフォリオマネージャー(portfolio manager)は国(country)ごとの配分(allocation)投資プロセスを持っている場合がある。この分析レベル(level of analysis)では、同じ配分公式(allocation formula)でマネージャー(manager)のポートフォリオ(portfolio)内での国配分決定(country allocation decisions)の影響を計算でき、選択公式(selection formula)で各国(country)内での選択決定(selection decisions)の影響を計算できる。

さらにもう一段階掘り下げると、ポートフォリオマネージャー(portfolio manager)は、各国(country)内で先に述べたようなセクター配分(sector allocation)プロセスを持っている場合があり、これにより、配分公式(allocation formula)で各国内でのセクター選択(sector selection)決定の影響や、セクター内での選択(selection)決定の影響を計算できる。

どの分析レベル(level of analysis)であっても、リターンアトリビューション(return attribution)はポートフォリオマネージャー(portfolio manager)の意思決定プロセス(decision process)を反映することが不可欠である。例えば、ユーロ圏(Eurozone)の投資戦略(investment strategy)は、各国(country)内での証券選択(security selection)を伴う国別配分(country allocation)プロセス、または各産業セクター(industrial sector)内での証券選択を伴うセクター配分(sector allocation)プロセスを使用する場合がある。展示図23(Exhibit 23)および展示図24(Exhibit 24)は、異なる投資プロセス(investment process)を想定した場合に、同じポートフォリオ(portfolio)から得られる可能性のある異なる分析結果(different analysis)を示す。いずれの場合も、算術的(arithmetic)なブリンソン・アンド・ファッヒラー(Brinson and Fachler)アプローチが使用されている。

 Exhibit 23Country Allocation
展示図23: 国別配分(Country Allocation)
ポートフォリオウェイト(Portfolio Weight) ベンチマークウェイト(Benchmark Weight) ポートフォリオリターン(Portfolio Return) ベンチマークリターン(Benchmark Return) 配分(Allocation) 選択(Selection)
フランス(France) 20% 30% 8.0% 6.0% 0.15% 0.40%
ドイツ(Germany) 20 35 8.0 7.0 0.07 0.20
オランダ(Holland) 20 10 9.0 15.0 0.76 −1.20
Italy 30 15 10.0 9.0 0.23 0.30
Spain 10 10 3.0 3.5 0.00 −0.05
Total 100% 100% 8.30 7.45% 1.20% −0.35%
 Exhibit 24Industry Sector Allocation
展示図24: 産業セクター配分(Industry Sector Allocation)
ポートフォリオウェイト(Portfolio Weight) Benchmark Weight ポートフォリオリターン(Portfolio Return) Benchmark Return Allocation 選択(Selection)
エネルギー(Energy) 25% 30% 18.0% 12.0% −0.23% 1.50%
ヘルスケア(Health Care) 30 20 −3.0 −6.0 −1.35 0.90
金融(Financial) 20 30 10.0 12.0 −0.46 −0.40
輸送(Transportation) 10 15 12.0 8.0 −0.03 0.40
金属・鉱業(Metals and Mining) 15 5 10.0 5.0 −0.25 0.75
Total 100% 100% 8.30% 7.45% −2.30% 3.15%

Exhibit 23 demonstrates good country allocation but negative security selection within countries, whereas Exhibit 24 demonstrates poor sector allocation but strongly positive security selection within industrial sectors. The message with regard to selection effects is completely different depending on the investment decision process used.

Drilling down to the lowest level, the same allocation and selection formulas can be used to calculate individual security decisions within sectors. For example, the allocation formula can determine the impact of over- or underweighting individual securities, whereas the selection formula will determine the contribution as a result of the returns of a security in the portfolio differing from the return of the same security in the benchmark. If the pricing sources used in the portfolio and the benchmark are identical, then any difference in return will be caused by transaction activity. Transaction activity as a result of trading expenses and bid–offer spreads will negatively affect returns, but very occasionally because of timing, the portfolio manager may be able to trade at advantageous prices during the day and recover all the transaction costs by the end of the day resulting in a positive effect.

Exhibit 25 shows the security level return attribution effects for a small portfolio of oil stocks against a customized benchmark consisting of the same oil stocks. This approach would be used by a pure stock picker, the only decisions in the portfolio being individual stock weighting and timing decisions.

 Exhibit 25Security Level Return Attribution Effects of Pure Stock Picker
Exhibit 25: Security Level Return Attribution Effects of Pure Stock Picker
Portfolio Weight Benchmark Weight Portfolio Return Benchmark Return Allocation Transaction Costs and Timing Effects
Chevron Corporation 24% 30% 10% 10% −0.18% 0.0%
Conoco Phillips 21% 25% 8% 8% −0.04% 0.0%
ExxonMobil 41% 35% 5% 6% −0.06% −0.41%
Marathon Oil 6% 5% 4% 4% −0.03% 0.0%
Newfield Exploration 8% 5% −5% −5% −0.36% 0.0%
Total 100% 100% 5.97% 7.05% −0.67% −0.41%

The arithmetic allocation effects of each security using the BF approach are as follows:

Chevron Corporation (24% – 30%) × (10% – 7.05%) = –0.18%
Conoco Phillips (21% – 25%) × (8.0% – 7.05%) = –0.04%
ExxonMobil (41% – 35%) × (6.0% – 7.05%) = –0.06%
Marathon Oil (6% – 5%) × (4.0% – 7.05%) = –0.03%
Newfield Exploration (8% – 5%) × (–5.0% – 7.05%) = –0.36%

Allocation in this context measures the value added from individual security selection. There are transactions in only one security during the period—ExxonMobil—therefore, there are selection effects (transaction costs and timing) in only this security. The calculation is

ExxonMobil 41% × (5.0% – 6.0%) = –0.41%

6 一部のポートフォリオ(portfolios)では、次のレベル(next level)が資産クラス(asset classes)である場合がある。

スポンサー(sponsor)レベルでのアトリビューション(attribution)

ポートフォリオマネージャー(portfolio manager)レベルでのアトリビューション(attribution)

☰ Index ← 前へ 1.11 | Return Attribution Analysis at Multiple Levels 次へ →
